questions.q: Communication required before entering Class D airspace?
choices: ATC clearance must be received
Two-way radio communication must be established with the controlling facility
Must file a flight plan
No communication required for VFR
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.129: Two-way radio communication must be established with the ATC facility responsible for Class D airspace before entry.

questions.q: Class B entry requires what specific clearance from ATC?
choices: Two-way radio communications established
Explicit ATC clearance such as cleared into Class Bravo plus Mode C transponder and ADS-B Out
Just a Mode C transponder
Filing a flight plan and squawking a code
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.131: Must receive explicit clearance. Simply establishing radio contact is NOT sufficient unlike Class C and D.

questions.q: Class A airspace begins at what altitude?
choices: 10000 ft MSL
14500 ft MSL
18000 ft MSL
FL600
questions.explanation: Class A airspace extends from 18000 ft MSL (FL180) to FL600. All operations within Class A must be conducted under IFR.

questions.q: Can VFR pilots legally enter an active MOA?
choices: No — ATC clearance required
Yes — no prohibition, but extreme caution advised; check FSS for activity status
Yes — no restrictions ever apply to VFR in MOAs
Only if flying below 1000 AGL
questions.explanation: MOAs do not require ATC clearance for VFR flight. However, pilots should exercise extreme caution and check activity status through FSS. When active, high-speed military maneuvers occur.

questions.q: Dashed magenta circle around a non-towered airport on a sectional indicates?
choices: Class D airspace
Class C airspace
Class E airspace starting at the surface associated with instrument approaches
An alert area boundary
questions.explanation: A dashed magenta circle indicates Class E airspace beginning at the surface, typically at airports with published instrument approaches but no operating control tower.

questions.q: Mode C Veil extends how far from a Class B primary airport?
choices: 10 nm
20 nm
30 nm
50 nm
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.215(b)(2): A transponder with Mode C is required within 30 nm of the Class B primary airport, from the surface up to 10000 ft MSL.

questions.q: Standard Class C airspace lateral dimensions?
choices: 10 nm radius surface to 10000
5 nm inner circle surface to 4000 AGL and 10 nm outer ring 1200 to 4000 AGL
20 nm radius surface to 5000
5 nm ring surface to 2500
questions.explanation: Standard Class C: 5 nm inner circle from surface to 4000 AGL, 10 nm outer ring from 1200 AGL to 4000 AGL.

questions.q: ADS-B Out required in which airspace?
choices: Class B only
Class A, B, C; Class E at and above 10000 MSL except below 2500 AGL; and above 3000 MSL over Gulf of Mexico
Only above FL180
Only in the Mode C veil
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.225: ADS-B Out required in Class A, B, C, Class E at or above 10000 MSL (except below 2500 AGL), and above 3000 MSL over the Gulf of Mexico.

questions.q: What is a Prohibited Area?
choices: Airspace requiring special equipment
Airspace where flight is prohibited at all times — examples include P-56 (White House) and P-49 (Camp David)
A Military Operations Area when active
An Alert Area with high-density traffic
questions.explanation: Prohibited areas are established for national security reasons where flight is prohibited at all times under 14 CFR 73. No civilian operations permitted.

questions.q: Class C airspace depicted on a sectional chart in what color?
choices: Blue
Magenta
Blue dashed
Magenta dashed
questions.explanation: Class C airspace is shown with solid magenta circles on sectional charts. Class D is depicted with segmented (dashed) blue circles.

questions.q: Speed limit for aircraft at or below 2500 AGL within 4 nm of a Class C or D airport?
choices: 250 KIAS
200 KIAS
175 KIAS
No specific limit
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.117(b): Below 2500 AGL within 4 nm of a Class C or D airport the speed limit is 200 KIAS.

questions.q: What is an Alert Area on a sectional chart?
choices: Airspace requiring ATC clearance
High volume of pilot training or unusual aerial activity — no clearance needed, all pilots equally responsible for collision avoidance
Military operations — restricted to military
Active restricted area
questions.explanation: Alert Areas contain high volumes of pilot training or unusual aerial activity. No ATC clearance is required. All aircraft equally responsible for collision avoidance.

questions.q: Control tower closes at 10 PM at a Class D airport. You arrive at 11 PM. What rules apply?
choices: Still Class D — use UNICOM for traffic advisories
Class D converts to Class E or Class G per the Chart Supplement — check before flying
Class D automatically becomes Class B when uncontrolled
No airspace restrictions apply after the tower closes
questions.explanation: When a control tower closes, Class D airspace reverts to either Class E or Class G depending on what the Chart Supplement indicates for that specific airport.

questions.q: When can a pilot legally enter a Restricted Area without prior ATC clearance?
choices: Never — always need prior clearance
Only if flying below 500 AGL
When the area is inactive and ATC confirms it is available for normal operations
Any time in VFR conditions
questions.explanation: When a Restricted Area is not active, the controlling agency releases it to ATC for normal use. ATC may advise pilots that the area is available. Always verify before entry.

questions.q: Class D airspace typically extends to what height AGL?
questions.explanation: Class D airspace typically extends from the surface to 2500 ft AGL. Exact boundaries for each airport are shown on sectional charts.

questions.q: You are 8 nm from a Class C primary airport at 2000 ft MSL with airport elevation 500 ft MSL. The outer ring floor is 1200 AGL. Are you inside Class C?
choices: No — you are outside the 10 nm outer ring
Yes — inside the outer ring above its 1200 AGL floor; must establish two-way comms before continuing
Yes — ATC clearance explicitly required to enter
No — the 5 nm inner circle is the only area requiring comms
questions.explanation: The outer ring extends 10 nm. At 8 nm you are inside it. At 2000 ft MSL with a 500 ft airport elevation, you are at 1500 ft AGL — above the 1200 AGL floor of the outer ring. Two-way comms must be established before entry.

questions.q: What is a Warning Area?
choices: Airspace that requires ATC clearance
Airspace over international waters (3+ nm from US coast) containing hazardous activities — VFR not prohibited but strongly discouraged
Prohibited area near sensitive government installations
MOA equivalent over international waters
questions.explanation: Warning Areas are established over international waters containing potentially hazardous activity. They extend from 3 nm outward. Non-participating aircraft are not prohibited but strongly advised to avoid.

questions.q: You inadvertently fly into an active Restricted Area. What should you do?
choices: Nothing — if VFR no enforcement action possible
Exit immediately, contact ATC, identify yourself and explain the situation. Be prepared for possible FAA enforcement action.
Only enforcement if you were IFR
File a NASA ASRS report and nothing else is required
questions.explanation: Unauthorized entry into a Restricted Area is an airspace violation. Exit immediately, contact ATC, and be prepared to file a report. FAA may take enforcement action. Filing a NASA ASRS report provides some protection but does not eliminate FAA action.

questions.q: What equipment is required to operate in Class A airspace?
choices: VFR equipment plus a transponder
IFR-equipped aircraft plus an operable Mode C transponder
Just a two-way radio
Transponder and ADS-B Out only
questions.explanation: Class A requires IFR equipment including suitable navigation equipment, plus an operable Mode C transponder. Operations must be under an IFR clearance.

questions.q: A TFR under 91.137(a)(1) — what type of operations does it primarily protect?
choices: Presidential movement
Space operations
Aircraft flying relief operations at or below 1000 AGL for immediate safety of individuals
Public entertainment events
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.137(a)(1): This type of TFR protects aircraft flying relief operations at or below 1000 AGL, law enforcement aircraft, and aircraft providing direct safety support.

questions.q: What document should always be checked for airspace that converts when a tower closes?
choices: Sectional aeronautical chart
Chart Supplement (formerly Airport/Facility Directory)
NOTAM system
AIM Chapter 3
questions.explanation: The Chart Supplement contains specific information about each airport including what the airspace converts to when the tower is closed and the hours of operation.

questions.q: ATC tells you to stay outside the Bravo. You have not received cleared into Class B. What does this mean?
choices: You are cleared into the Class B but at low altitude
You do NOT have clearance to enter Class B and must remain outside its boundaries
You may transit at or below the floor of the Class B
ATC will guide you through on VFR flight following
questions.explanation: Without hearing the words cleared into Class Bravo airspace or a similar explicit clearance, you do not have authorization to enter Class B.

questions.q: What is an SFRA and where does one exist in the US?
choices: Standard Flight Rules Area — applies everywhere
Special Flight Rules Area — the Washington DC area (DC SFRA) requires specific training and authorization to operate within
Simplified Flight Route Authorization — for commercial operators
Seasonal Flight Restriction Area — applied during wildfire season
questions.explanation: An SFRA imposes specific requirements beyond standard FARs. The Washington DC SFRA (within 30 nm of DCA) requires specific online training and an ATC authorization or flight plan to operate within.

questions.q: Solid blue segmented (dashed) circle on a sectional chart depicts what?
choices: Class B airspace
Class C airspace
Class D airspace surface area
Class E airspace at the surface
questions.explanation: Class D airspace is depicted with a segmented (dashed) blue circle on sectional charts.

questions.q: A magenta vignette (shaded boundary) on a sectional indicates Class E airspace starting at what altitude?
choices: Surface
700 ft AGL (within the shaded area)
1200 ft AGL (outside the shaded area)
2500 ft AGL
questions.explanation: The magenta shaded area indicates Class E transition airspace beginning at 700 ft AGL. Outside the shaded area, Class E typically starts at 1200 AGL.

questions.q: What is the floor of Class E airspace in most controlled areas en route?
choices: Surface everywhere
700 ft AGL near airports with instrument approaches; 1200 ft AGL in most other areas
2500 ft AGL
Only above FL180
questions.explanation: Class E airspace has several different floors depending on the area. Near airports with instrument approaches: 700 ft AGL (shown by magenta shading on sectionals). En-route over most of the continental US: 1200 ft AGL. In some areas it begins at the surface.

questions.q: What is the difference between an airspace clearance and two-way communication in Class B vs Class C?
choices: There is no difference — all controlled airspace including Class B, C, and D requires explicit verbal clearance from ATC before entry; two-way radio contact alone does not constitute authorization to enter any class of controlled airspace

Class B requires explicit ATC clearance (must hear 'cleared into the Bravo'). Class C requires only two-way communication to be established (ATC acknowledging your callsign). Never enter Class B without explicit clearance.

Class C requires explicit ATC clearance (must hear 'cleared into the Charlie'). Class B requires only two-way communication to be established (ATC acknowledging your callsign). You may enter Class B with only radio contact confirmed.

Both Class B and Class C require only two-way radio communication to be established — ATC does not need to issue an explicit clearance for either; the pilot need only ensure ATC has acknowledged the aircraft by callsign before entry
questions.explanation: This is a critical distinction: Class B requires explicit clearance language from ATC (cleared into Class B airspace). Class C requires only that two-way communication is established — ATC does not have to issue a clearance, but they must acknowledge your aircraft by callsign. Never enter Class B without explicit clearance.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a NOTAM and where can they be obtained?
choices: Notice to Air Missions — provides time-critical aeronautical information that is temporary in nature or not known far enough in advance to be published — available through 1800wxbrief.com, ATIS, ForeFlight, and other FAA-authorized sources

Notice to Airspace Managers — provides permanent regulatory changes to controlled airspace dimensions and class designations — distributed monthly by the FAA to all certificated pilots through the Airman's Information Manual updates.

Notice to Airport Managers — provides long-term infrastructure advisories about runway construction and facility upgrades — published quarterly in the Airport Facility Directory and accessible through standard EFB applications.

Notice to All Mechanics — provides mandatory airworthiness directives and service bulletin alerts for certificated aircraft — issued by the FAA and required reading for all maintenance personnel before performing scheduled inspections.
questions.explanation: NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions) provide time-critical aeronautical information about airport closures, runway closures, TFRs, GPS outages, NAVAID outages, and other temporary conditions. VFR pilots must check NOTAMs before every flight. Available at 1800wxbrief.com, ForeFlight, Garmin Pilot, and directly from FSS.

questions.q: What is the DC SFRA and what specific requirements does it impose on VFR pilots?
choices: The Washington DC Special Flight Rules Area does not apply to VFR flights below 1500 ft AGL; only IFR aircraft operating in the 30 nm ring need an ATC clearance, a filed flight plan, and a discrete transponder code.

The Washington DC Special Flight Rules Area (30 nm of DCA): requires completion of an FAA online course, an assigned discrete transponder code, two-way radio, and filing a specific SFRA flight plan or having an ATC authorization before entry

The Washington DC Air Defense Identification Zone (30 nm of DCA): requires a current IFR rating, an ATC clearance, Mode C transponder, and a filed IFR flight plan — VFR operations are entirely prohibited within the outer ring.

The Washington DC Terminal Control Area (25 nm of DCA): requires an operable Mode C transponder and ATC clearance for entry but no prior training course or flight plan filing for VFR aircraft operating below 3000 ft MSL.
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 93.331: The DC SFRA (30 nm of DCA, surface to FL180) requires: (1) completion of the FAA online SFRA training course, (2) filing an SFRA flight plan or having an ATC authorization, (3) squawking a discrete ATC-assigned transponder code, (4) two-way radio communication. VFR pilots must also comply with the DC ADIZ requirements.

questions.q: Under what conditions can a pilot operate below 10,000 ft MSL in Class A airspace?
choices: Any time with ATC permission
Never — all Class A operations must be under IFR at or above 18,000 ft MSL
Only for military training
Only with a type rating for the aircraft
questions.explanation: Class A airspace is defined as 18,000 ft MSL to FL600. All operations within Class A must be conducted under IFR, by an instrument-rated pilot, with an appropriate IFR clearance. There are no VFR operations in Class A.

questions.q: What is a VFR flyway and how does it relate to Class B airspace?
choices: A VFR flyway is a published route through (not around) Class C airspace that allows VFR aircraft to transit a busy terminal area without ATC clearance — shown on sectional charts as dotted lines with mandatory altitude assignments

A VFR flyway is a published route around (not through) Class B airspace that allows VFR aircraft to transit an area without entering the Bravo — shown on TAC charts as dashed lines with recommended altitudes

A VFR flyway is a published route below (not through) Class B airspace that requires ATC clearance for VFR aircraft — shown on TAC charts as solid lines with maximum altitudes to remain clear of the Bravo floor

A VFR flyway is a published route around Class D airspace that allows IFR aircraft to transition a terminal area without contacting tower — depicted on approach plates as thick dashed lines with assigned transition altitudes
questions.explanation: VFR flyways (also called VFR corridors in some contexts) are published on VFR Terminal Area Charts (TAC). They are recommended routes around Class B airspace for VFR aircraft to avoid the Bravo without needing clearance. They should not be confused with VFR corridors through Class B (which do exist at some locations).

questions.q: What does it mean when a sectional chart shows a dashed blue circle with an altitude number such as [27] inside it?
choices: The maximum altitude for VFR flight in that area
The floor of Class E airspace in hundreds of feet MSL (e.g., [27] = 2700 ft MSL) — shown as the top of Class G beneath it
The MEF for the quadrangle
The maximum transponder altitude for that sector
questions.explanation: Dashed blue circles (step-down fixes or airspace segments) with altimeter numbers in brackets or parentheses indicate the floor of Class E airspace in MSL at that point. This is important when transitioning between airspace classes vertically.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Class B VFR corridor, where one exists?
choices: A high-speed lane reserved for turbine aircraft transiting beneath Class B, requiring ATC clearance and Mode C transponder but no explicit Bravo clearance — used at specified altitudes between designated outer marker fixes

A specific designated passage through Class B airspace that VFR aircraft may use without ATC clearance — at specified altitudes and typically following a specific route such as between two outer rings
A charted transition route around (not through) Class B airspace for VFR pilots who cannot obtain a Bravo clearance — flown at published altitudes following a specific shoreline or highway corridor

A published practice area designated within Class C airspace for student solo flights — restricted to specified altitudes and a circular route connecting two outer fixes without requiring approach control permission to remain
questions.explanation: At some Class B airports, specific VFR corridors through (not around) the Bravo airspace are established. These allow VFR aircraft to transit the area at specific altitudes without requiring a clearance — but the pilot must remain within the corridor boundaries. Not all Class B airports have these.

questions.q: What is the lateral dimension of the Mode C veil and what equipment does it require?
choices: 10 nm from the Class B primary airport — requires only a transponder
30 nm from the Class B primary airport up to 10,000 ft MSL — requires a transponder with Mode C capability AND ADS-B Out (both required simultaneously)
20 nm from any Class B airport — requires Mode C only
50 nm from any Class B airport — requires full IFR equipment
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.215 and 91.225: The Mode C veil extends 30 nm from the Class B primary airport, from the surface up to 10,000 ft MSL. Both an operable Mode C transponder AND ADS-B Out are required in this area.

questions.q: What are the minimum requirements to operate in Class G airspace?
choices: Transponder and ADS-B Out
Two-way radio communication
Virtually no requirements — Class G is uncontrolled airspace where basic VFR rules (visibility and cloud clearance) still apply, but no communications or equipment are required
At least a student pilot certificate
questions.explanation: Class G is uncontrolled airspace. No ATC communication, clearance, or special equipment is required. However, all VFR weather minimums (visibility and cloud clearance) still apply. See-and-avoid responsibilities remain in effect.

questions.q: What is an ADIZ and what are the requirements for VFR flight through it?
choices: Air Defense Identification Zone — only IFR flights are affected; VFR pilots have no special requirements when transiting coastal ADIZs provided they remain in contact with the nearest ARTCC and maintain VFR cloud clearances throughout

Air Defense Identification Zone — VFR pilots must file a DVFR (Defense VFR) flight plan, two-way radio, operable transponder, and must depart within 5 minutes of filed time or notify ATC before deviating from the filed plan

Air Defense Identification Zone — all pilots must obtain a Letter of Authorization from the FAA, carry two-way radio, operable transponder, and must depart within 30 minutes of filed time or file an amended plan with ATC

Air Defense Identification Zone — VFR pilots must file a standard VFR flight plan, squawk 7700 on entry, maintain two-way radio on guard frequency 121.5 throughout, and must cross the boundary within 5 minutes of the filed estimate
questions.explanation: ADIZs (Air Defense Identification Zones) are established for national security purposes, particularly along the US coasts. VFR pilots must file a DVFR flight plan, maintain two-way radio communication, have an operable transponder, and depart within 5 minutes of the filed departure time.

questions.q: When a control tower is in operation what is the significance of a flashing green light signal to an aircraft on the ground?
choices: Stop
Cleared to takeoff
Cleared to taxi
Return to starting point on airport
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.125 / AIM 4-3-13: Light gun signals to aircraft on the ground. Flashing green = cleared to taxi. Steady green = cleared for takeoff. Flashing red = taxi clear of runway. Steady red = stop. Alternating red and green = exercise extreme caution.

questions.q: What does a steady red light signal from the tower mean to an aircraft in flight?
choices: Give way to other aircraft and continue circling
Do not land — airport unsafe or closed
Continue circling, airport not clear for landing
Return to starting point on airport
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.125 / AIM 4-3-13: Light gun signals to aircraft in flight. Steady red = give way to other aircraft and continue circling. Flashing red = airport unsafe — do not land. Flashing green = return for landing. Steady green = cleared to land.

questions.q: What is a Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) under 14 CFR 91.143 and when is it used?
choices: A permanent airspace restriction
A TFR for space operations (rocket launches) that temporarily restricts airspace to protect the public from hazards associated with space flight operations
A TFR for presidential movement only
A TFR for military training areas
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.143: This specific type of TFR is established for space flight operations to protect airspace during rocket launches, space vehicle ascents and descents. The TFR defines the restricted area and time period around launch/landing sites.

questions.q: What is the definition of Class E airspace at the surface and where does it commonly exist?
choices: Class E airspace that begins at 700 ft AGL (depicted by magenta vignette shading) — commonly established around airports with instrument approaches to funnel IFR traffic from the en-route structure down to the airport environment.

Class E airspace that begins at the surface (depicted by dashed magenta circles) — commonly established at airports with published instrument procedures but no operating control tower, or at some airports with part-time towers

Class E airspace that begins at 1200 ft AGL (depicted by blue vignette shading) — commonly established as the default controlled airspace across most of the contiguous US to protect IFR aircraft in cruise and transition flight.

Class E airspace that begins at 18,000 ft MSL (depicted by a thick blue border on sectionals) — established as Class E high airspace to separate domestic jet routes from oceanic traffic operating under ICAO procedures.
questions.explanation: Class E surface areas extend from the surface and are depicted with dashed magenta circles on sectional charts. They are commonly established at airports with published instrument approaches (often ILS or RNAV approaches) where instrument protection down to the surface is needed but no control tower operates.
